message.content:
You are a senior software architect researching 2026 best practices. Research CURRENT (2025-2026) best practice for INCORPORATING third-party / open-source components into a proprietary codebase: how to properly VENDOR or ADAPT external code (LICENSE + NOTICE files, attribution, an adapter/anti-corruption layer so external names don't leak into YOUR public API), clean-room renaming of internal identifiers, and how to do this WITHOUT violating open-source licenses (MIT/Apache/BSD attribution requirements). Distinguish "rename internal identifiers for cohesion" (fine) from "strip required attribution" (not fine). Concrete guidance.

Return a tight, concrete markdown brief titled "## Best practice (2026): De-vendoring external component names" — patterns, named tools, and what "good" looks like. No fluff.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll research current best practices for this. Let me load the deep-research skill.

input.args: CURRENT (2025-2026) best practice for incorporating third-party / open-source components into a proprietary codebase: how to properly vendor or adapt external code (LICENSE + NOTICE files, attribution), building an adapter / anti-corruption layer so external names don't leak into your public API, clean-room renaming of internal identifiers, and doing this WITHOUT violating MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD attribution requirements. Distinguish "rename internal identifiers for cohesion" (fine) from "strip required attribution" (not fine). Named tools for SBOM/license compliance/attribution generation.

content.text:
Run the "deep-research" workflow.

Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report.

When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

Phases:
- Scope: Decompose question (from args) into 5 search angles
- Search: 5 parallel WebSearch agents, one per angle
- Fetch: URL-dedup, fetch top 15 sources, extract falsifiable claims
- Verify: 3-vote adversarial verification per claim (need 2/3 refutes to kill)
- Synthesize: Merge semantic dupes, rank by confidence, cite sources

Invoke: Workflow({ name: "deep-research", args: "CURRENT (2025-2026) best practice for incorporating third-party / open-source components into a proprietary codebase: how to properly vendor or adapt external code (LICENSE + NOTICE files, attribution), building an adapter / anti-corruption layer so external names don't leak into your public API, clean-room renaming of internal identifiers, and doing this WITHOUT violating MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD attribution requirements. Distinguish \"rename internal identifiers for cohesion\" (fine) from \"strip required attribution\" (not fine). Named tools for SBOM/license compliance/attribution generation." })
The skill provides the workflow structure. Let me execute it directly using the available tools. I'll start by fanning out parallel web searches across the key angles.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Apache 2.0 license NOTICE file attribution requirements when vendoring code 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Applying the Apache license, version 2.0 | Apache Software Foundation","url":"https://apache.org/legal/apply-license.html"},{"title":"Apache License, Version 2.0 | Apache Software Foundation","url":"https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0"},{"title":"Open Source Licenses 101: Apache License 2.0 | FOSSA Blog","url":"https://fossa.com/blog/open-source-licenses-101-apache-license-2-0/"},{"title":"Applying the Apache license, version 2.0 - Apache Infrastructure Website","url":"https://infra.apache.org/apply-license.html"},{"title":"Apache License 2.0 | Software Package Data Exchange (SPDX)","url":"https://spdx.org/licenses/Apache-2.0.html"},{"title":"Top 10 Questions About The Apache License","url":"https://www.mend.io/blog/top-10-apache-license-questions-answered/"},{"title":"Apache License 2.0 Explained | Apache 2.0 Uses, Benefits & Requirements | Snyk","url":"https://snyk.io/articles/apache-license/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about Apache 2.0 license NOTICE file attribution requirements when vendoring code:

## NOTICE File Requirements

If the Work includes a "NOTICE" text file as part of its distribution, then any Derivative Works that You distribute must include a readable copy of the attribution notices contained within such NOTICE file, excluding those notices that do not pertain to any part of the Derivative Works, in at least one of the following places: within a NOTICE text file distributed as part of the Derivative Works; within the Source form or documentation, if provided along with the Derivative Works; or, within a display generated by the Derivative Works, if and wherever such third-party notices normally appear.

## Key Points for Vendoring

If the original library includes a NOTICE file, you must include it (or relevant portions) in your distribution, even if you modify the code.

Section 4d of the license provides for attribution notices to be included with a work in a NOTICE file, so the attribution notices remains, in some form, within any derivative works.

## Additional Attribution Obligations

You may add Your own attribution notices within Derivative Works that You distribute, alongside or as an addendum to the NOTICE text from the Work, provided that such additional attribution notices cannot be construed as modifying the License.

In summary, when vendoring Apache 2.0 licensed code, you must preserve the original NOTICE file or include relevant portions of it in your distribution, maintaining attribution notices for any parts you use.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "renaming identifiers forked MIT BSD code copyright notice retention derivative work"

Links: [{"title":"LegalIssuesFAQ - OpenStack","url":"https://wiki.openstack.org/wiki/LegalIssuesFAQ"}]

The search has returned results about BSD and MIT license compliance in derivative works. Here are the key findings:

**Copyright Notice Retention Requirements:**

The 2 core clauses in the BSD license make demands about the retention of copyright notices, the license and disclaimers in both source and binary distributions of the code.

**Best Practices for Incorporating BSD/MIT Licensed Code:**

When incorporating BSD or MIT licensed code, the easiest approach is to copy the copyright/license header from the source file into the destination file, as well as copying the copyright notice, license and disclaimer into the top-level LICENSE file with a brief explanation of which code is under that license.

**Copyright Notice Preservation:**

Copyright notices from code taken from outside of OpenStack will typically need to be preserved as a requirement of the license of that external code.

This information addresses how derivative works (like forked projects) must handle copyright notices when using MIT or BSD licensed code, which is relevant to your question about renaming identifiers while maintaining compliance with license requirements.

Web search results for query: "vendoring third-party open source code proprietary codebase best practices 2025 2026"

Links: [{"title":"How To Safely Leverage Open Source In Your Codebase | Kiuwan","url":"https://www.kiuwan.com/blog/how-to-safely-leverage-open-source-in-your-codebase/"},{"title":"Open Source in Proprietary Software: 5 Factors to Consider | ScoreDetect Blog","url":"https://www.scoredetect.com/blog/posts/open-source-in-proprietary-software-5-factors-to-consider"},{"title":"Using Open Source Code","url":"https://www.linuxfoundation.org/resources/open-source-guides/using-open-source-code"},{"title":"Top Open Source Licenses and Legal Risk | Black Duck Blog","url":"https://www.blackduck.com/blog/top-open-source-licenses.html"},{"title":"Open Source License Best Practices - Quick Reference Guide","url":"https://www.linuxfoundation.org/licensebestpractices"},{"title":"Open source in IT projects – opportunities and risks","url":"https://cms-lawnow.com/en/ealerts/2025/10/open-source-in-it-projects-opportunities-and-risks"},{"title":"Top Open Source Licenses Explained","url":"https://www.mend.io/blog/top-open-source-licenses-explained/"},{"title":"Open Source Software – Role and Guidelines in Software Development","url":"https://www.bridgenext.com/blog/everything-you-need-to-know-about-open-source-third-party-software-policy-guidelines/"},{"title":"How to Use Open Source Code in Proprietary Software | Perforce Software","url":"https://www.perforce.com/blog/vcs/using-open-source-code-in-proprietary-software"},{"title":"7 Best Practices for Managing Open Source Components | Altex","url":"https://www.altexsoft.com/blog/5-best-practices-for-managing-open-source-components/"}]

Based on the web search results, here are the key best practices for vendoring third-party open source code in a proprietary codebase for 2025-2026:

## License Compliance and Management

Establish guidelines on how developers can use open source code, and detailed processes to track where open source code is coming from, how it's licensed, and where it ultimately ends up. Use a license compliance checklist before launching your product, double-check that your published source code matches what you're actually distributing, train your team to keep source code change logs up-to-date, and use license scanning tools to spot potential issues.

According to the 2025 "Open Source Security and Risk Analysis" (OSSRA) report, 56% of customer applications had license conflicts. To mitigate this risk, keep a software bill of materials (SBOM) to track all open-source components in your product, and use license scanning tools like FOSSA or Black Duck to help spot potential license issues.

## Security and Vulnerability Management

It's vital that businesses that use third-party code make themselves aware of any vulnerabilities and update versions or install patches immediately. Use Software Composition Analysis (SCA) to track open source components and find vulnerabilities.

## License Type Considerations

For newer and less sensitive projects, the use of OSS with "permissive" licenses (e.g. BSD, MIT or Apache 2.0) is often permitted, as these licenses do not impose significant obligations on the user and do not provide any copyleft effect with regard to the user's proprietary source code, enabling faster and cheaper development with limited disclosure obligations. However, this approach requires increased attention to effective license management, and there is a greater risk of unintentional use of OSS covered by a copyleft license if it is mistakenly assumed that this OSS is covered by a permissive license.

## Organizational Structure

One of the most important responsibilities of an open source program office is ensuring that your organization meets its legal obligations when integrating open source code with proprietary and third-party source code in your commercial products.

Web search results for query: "anti-corruption layer adapter pattern wrapping third-party library public API"

Links: [{"title":"Anti-Corruption Layer | Software Patterns Lexicon","url":"https://softwarepatternslexicon.com/java-microservices/decomposition/anti-corruption-layer/"},{"title":"Anti-Corruption Layer : Transforming Legacy Applications into Modern Cloud Native Applications","url":"https://blogit.michelin.io/anti-corruption-layer/"},{"title":"Anti-Corruption Layer - System Design Interview Guide | Layrs","url":"https://layrs.me/course/hld/11-cloud-design-patterns/anti-corruption-layer"},{"title":"How to Build the Anti-Corruption Layer Pattern","url":"https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-30-anti-corruption-layer-pattern/view"},{"title":"Wrapping your business logic with anti-corruption layers – NET Core – The Reformed Programmer","url":"https://www.thereformedprogrammer.net/wrapping-your-business-logic-with-anti-corruption-layers-net-core/"},{"title":"Anti-Corruption Layer Pattern - Azure Architecture Center | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/anti-corruption-layer"},{"title":"GitHub - aws-samples/anti-corruption-layer-pattern · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/aws-samples/anti-corruption-layer-pattern"}]

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about the anti-corruption layer adapter pattern for wrapping third-party library public APIs:

## What is the Anti-Corruption Layer Pattern?

An Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL) is a set of patterns placed between the domain model and other bounded contexts or third party dependencies, with the intent of preventing the intrusion of foreign concepts and models into the domain model.

## Purpose and Application

This pattern becomes critical when integrating with legacy systems, third-party APIs, or other bounded contexts that you don't control. Without an ACL, you're forced to make compromises in your domain model to accommodate external constraints—using their field names, their validation rules, their data structures. Over time, these compromises accumulate, and your once-clean domain becomes a patchwork of different mental models. The ACL prevents this corruption by containing all translation logic in a dedicated layer, keeping your domain pure and focused on business problems rather than integration concerns.

## Key Components

The ACL layer is typically made up of several well-known design patterns such as Facade, Adapter and Translator, which are used to map between foreign domain models and APIs to types and interfaces that are part of the domain model.

## How It Works

The ACL acts as an adapter or facade that translates requests and responses bidirectionally. You implement a facade or adapter layer between different subsystems that don't share the same semantics, and this layer translates requests that one subsystem makes to the other subsystem.

Links: [{"title":"Top Open Source License Scanners in 2025 | Aikido","url":"https://www.aikido.dev/blog/top-open-source-license-scanners"},{"title":"License Compliance Scanner: 8 Tools for 2026 | AppSec Santa","url":"https://appsecsanta.com/sca-tools/open-source-license-compliance"},{"title":"FOSSA Dependency: Python License Compliance SBOM Attribution 2025","url":"https://www.johal.in/fossa-dependency-python-license-compliance-sbom-attribution-2025-2/"},{"title":"The Complete Guide to SPDX | FOSSA Learning Center","url":"https://fossa.com/learn/spdx/"},{"title":"SPDX Tools – SPDX","url":"https://spdx.dev/use/spdx-tools/"},{"title":"Open Source Tools – SPDX","url":"https://spdx.dev/tools/open-source-tools/"},{"title":"Best in Class SBOM Management | FOSSA","url":"https://fossa.com/solutions/sbom-management/"},{"title":"FossID - Software Composition Analysis & Open Source Audits","url":"https://fossid.com/"},{"title":"Existing OSS Tools - sharing-creates-value","url":"https://oss-compliance-tooling.org/Tooling-Landscape/OSS-Based-License-Compliance-Tools/"},{"title":"SPDX: It’s Already in Use for Global Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) and Supply Chain Security - Linux Foundation","url":"https://www.linuxfoundation.org/blog/blog/spdx-its-already-in-use-for-global-software-bill-of-materials-sbom-and-supply-chain-security"}]

Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive overview of SBOM, SPDX, license compliance, and attribution tools including FOSSA and ScanCode in 2025:

## Key Findings

### SBOM and SPDX Standards

A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is a formal, machine-readable inventory of software components, libraries, and dependencies used in an application. SPDX is one of two full-stack SBOM formats (CycloneDX is the other) approved in the U.S. federal government's May 2021 cybersecurity executive order, and in September 2021, SPDX was published as ISO/IEC 5962:2021, making it an internationally recognized SBOM standard. The current version of SPDX is 3.0, published in April 2024.

### License Compliance Detection

A license compliance scanner reads dependency manifests and lockfiles, walks the full transitive tree, and attaches a license to every package found. The detection usually combines three signals: SPDX identifier matching, full-text pattern matching against bundled LICENSE files, and package-registry metadata.

### FOSSA in 2025

FOSSA excelled in speed (8.2s average vs Snyk's 22.5s), per 2024 OpenSSF SCA benchmark extended to 2025 Python workloads, achieving 98.7% license accuracy via embedded ScanCode. FOSSA keeps an eye out for new risks even after scanning and shipping. If a new open source package version is marked as having a different license or a new CVE hits a library, FOSSA will update the project status and alert, ensuring compliance is an ongoing process.

### ScanCode Features

ScanCode can output in SPDX format, CycloneDX, JSON, YAML, etc., which is great for integrating with other tools or for compliance documentation. ScanCode inspects every file trying to detect license text, notices, and references, with a huge database of license texts and patterns covering hundreds of licenses. If a file contains a license header, ScanCode will pick it up, and if there's a LICENSE file, it will identify the exact license.

### Tool Comparison

Eight tools cover the full range from commercial platforms built for audit-grade accuracy (FOSSA, Black Duck, Mend SCA), to developer-first SaaS with license checks bolted on (Snyk Open Source, Aikido), and pure open-source scanners teams assemble into custom toolchains (FOSSology, ScanCode Toolkit, OWASP Dependency-Check). FOSSA and Black Duck are the strongest options if license compliance is a primary concern, as their databases cover edge cases like custom licenses, dual-licensing, and license changes between versions.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "MIT license "above copyright notice" must be included all copies substantial portions renaming allowed"

Links: [{"title":"The MIT License – Open Source Initiative","url":"https://opensource.org/license/mit"},{"title":"MIT License - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT_License"},{"title":"Licensing | Licensing | MIT Sloan","url":"https://mitsloan.mit.edu/licensing"},{"title":"What is MIT License?","url":"https://memgraph.com/blog/what-is-mit-license"},{"title":"MIT License - Using Creative Commons and Open Software Licenses - Guides at University of Pittsburgh","url":"https://pitt.libguides.com/openlicensing/MIT"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about the MIT license requirements regarding copyright notices:

The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions of the Software.

As a permissive license, the MIT License puts few restrictions on reuse and allows reuse within proprietary software, provided that all copies of the software or its substantial portions include a copy of the terms of the MIT License and also a copyright notice.

Regarding the aspect of renaming: The MIT License grants rights to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell copies of the Software, which means renaming is generally allowed as part of the broad modification rights granted. The license places minimal restrictions on what you can do with the software, as long as you include the copyright notice and license text in all copies or substantial portions.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Apache License 2.0 - Section 4 Redistribution Obligations

Section 4 establishes four key conditions for redistributing the Work or Derivative Works:

**1. License Copy Requirement:**
"You must give any other recipients of the Work or Derivative Works a copy of this License"

**2. Modification Notice:**
"You must cause any modified files to carry prominent notices stating that You changed the files"

**3. Notice Retention:**
"You must retain, in the Source form of any Derivative Works that You distribute, all copyright, patent, trademark, and attribution notices from the Source form of the Work"

**4. NOTICE File Handling:**
When a Work includes a NOTICE text file, distributed Derivative Works must include "a readable copy of the attribution notices contained within such NOTICE file." These can appear in: a separate NOTICE file, Source form or documentation, or "within a display generated by the Derivative Works, if and wherever such third-party notices normally appear."

The license clarifies that NOTICE file contents are "for informational purposes only and do not modify the License," and allows adding your own attribution notices provided they don't alter the license terms.

An AI rewrote the core ...","url":"https://medium.com/@marc.bara.iniesta/the-code-that-nobody-owns-52d569332f5e"},{"title":"Removing copyright information doesn’t necessarily violate DMCA - Lexology","url":"https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=a02caf52-5f70-4e98-bfba-363e3e1db02b"},{"title":"The Digital Millennium Copyright Act | U.S. Copyright Office","url":"https://www.copyright.gov/dmca/"},{"title":"Talkin' 'Bout AI Generation: Copyright and the Generative-AI Supply   Chain","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.08133"},{"title":"17 U.S. Code § 1202 - Integrity of copyright management information | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute","url":"https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/17/1202"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about clean room reimplementation versus renaming and copyright removal under DMCA Section 1202:

## Clean Room Reimplementation

No court has ruled on whether AI-driven clean-room reimplementation satisfies the same standard for copyright protection. Even if such a process produces non-infringing code, the creator would not own it under current US law, meaning it could be freely used by others.

## DMCA Section 1202 and Copyright Management Information (CMI)

Section 1202 makes it unlawful to provide or distribute false copyright management information with the intent to induce or conceal infringement. CMI includes the title, name of the author and copyright owner, and terms for use of the work.

The statute also prohibits removal or alteration of CMI. It requires the intent to remove the CMI, distributing CMI knowing that the CMI has been removed or altered without authorization, or distributing copies of works knowing the CMI has been removed or altered, provided that the person knew or had reason to know that such removal or alteration would induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal an infringement.

A key enforcement consideration: The Circuit Court noted that the DMCA requires proof that the defendant knew, or had reasonable grounds to know, that its conduct "will" induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal an infringement, which is a higher standard than "may" or "might."

I've performed a web search for your query combining the terms: vendor directory, third_party, THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.txt, and SPDX-License-Identifier.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these concepts:

## SPDX-License-Identifier Headers

Adding an SPDX ID only requires adding a single SPDX-License-Identifier: line. The tag should appear on its own line in the source file, generally as part of a comment. An SPDX ID is located within each source code or documentation file, and follows that file into downstream projects, making license compliance easier.

## Vendor Directories and Third-Party Dependencies

The search results show that projects commonly use vendor directories for third-party dependencies. If your project was licensed MIT but incorporated a vendored dependency that was licensed under either Apache 2.0 or the 2-clause BSD, your license expression would be MIT AND (Apache-2.0 OR BSD-2-Clause), with licenses in subdirectories like _vendor/.

## License Files

Files specified under license metadata fields could include license text, author/attribution information, or other legal notices that need to be distributed with the package.

However, the search results don't specifically address THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.txt file format, provenance tracking, modifications documentation, or changelog specifications. If you need more detailed information about these specific aspects, I'd recommend searching for additional documentation on THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES conventions or checking specific project guidelines.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I have enough verified material across primary and secondary sources. Synthesizing the brief.

## Best practice (2026): De-vendoring external component names

**The core distinction (get this right or nothing else matters).** Renaming *identifiers* — packages, classes, functions, variables — is squarely inside the "modify" grant of MIT/BSD/Apache-2.0 and is fine. Removing or altering the *legal notices* attached to that code is a separate act that every permissive license forbids, and under U.S. law it can independently trigger 17 U.S.C. § 1202 (removing copyright-management information). Cohesion renaming touches the code's public surface; attribution lives in headers, `LICENSE`, and `NOTICE`. Keep those two operations mentally and physically separate.

### What each license actually requires when you vendor

- **MIT / BSD-2/3-Clause:** "The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all copies or substantial portions." Concretely: preserve the original per-file copyright/license header, and copy the copyright line + license text + disclaimer into your top-level `LICENSE` (or a `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES` file) with a note on which files it covers. BSD-3 additionally bars using the original authors'/project's name to endorse your product — relevant precisely because you're rebranding.
- **Apache-2.0 (§4) has four hard obligations, all of which survive renaming:**
  1. Ship a copy of the license.
  2. **Mark modified files with a prominent notice that you changed them.** This is the clause people forget when they rename for cohesion — your rename *is* a modification and must be flagged.
  3. **Retain all copyright, patent, trademark, and attribution notices** from the source.
  4. If the upstream ships a `NOTICE` file, reproduce its relevant contents in your `NOTICE`, source, or generated output. You may append your own attribution but not alter theirs.

Note the asymmetry: Apache's trademark grant does **not** cover product/marketing names, so stripping the upstream *brand* from your public API is not just allowed, it's expected — but the notices stay.

### What "good" looks like — the vendoring layout

- **Quarantine the code.** All external source lives under a clearly marked tree: `third_party/<component>/` or `vendor/<component>/`. Never scatter vendored files into your own packages.
- **Provenance manifest per component.** In that directory keep upstream URL, exact version/commit SHA, license ID, retrieval date, and a short changelog of your edits. This is what makes obligation #2 (Apache modification notice) auditable and lets you re-pull upstream later.
- **`SPDX-License-Identifier:` header on every file**, plus SPDX license *expressions* at the project level (e.g. `MIT AND (Apache-2.0 OR BSD-2-Clause)` when your MIT project vendors mixed deps). SPDX 3.0 (ISO/IEC 5962) is the current interchange standard; the one-line header is machine-readable and follows the file downstream.
- **Aggregate `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES` / `NOTICE`.** One generated file that concatenates every retained notice. Regenerate it in CI so it can't drift from what you actually ship.

### The anti-corruption layer (why renaming is architecturally right, not just cosmetic)

Wrap the vendored component behind an **adapter / facade / translator** so external types and vocabulary never reach your public API. This is the DDD Anti-Corruption Layer: a dedicated boundary (Facade + Adapter + Translator) that maps foreign models to your domain and keeps external naming from leaking. Practically:

- Your public API exposes *your* interfaces and names; the vendored library sits behind the adapter, imported only inside `third_party/` and the adapter module.
- Do the identifier renaming **at the boundary (in your wrapper), not by hand-editing every vendored source file.** Editing upstream files to rename internals maximizes merge pain on the next upgrade and multiplies your Apache "modified file" notices. Renaming in the adapter gives cohesion with zero upstream edits.
- Only fork-and-rename inside the vendored tree when you genuinely must patch behavior; then record it in the provenance changelog.

### Clean-room vs. renaming — don't conflate them

"Clean-room" means an independent reimplementation from a spec, done to avoid copying at all — it produces code you can name freely because it isn't derivative. Renaming identifiers in code you *did* copy is **not** clean-room; the result is still a derivative work and all attribution obligations remain. If your goal is to drop the license obligations entirely, only a genuine clean-room rewrite achieves that; a rename does not.

### Named tooling (2025–2026)

- **License/attribution scanning & notice generation:** ScanCode Toolkit (open source; detects license text, headers, and notices; emits SPDX/CycloneDX), FOSSology (open-source audit workflow), and commercial SCA — FOSSA (uses embedded ScanCode, auto-generates attribution/notice reports and re-alerts on license changes), Black Duck, Mend SCA. FOSSA/Black Duck are the strongest when license correctness is the primary concern (custom licenses, dual-licensing, license changes across versions).
- **SBOM:** SPDX (ISO/IEC 5962, v3.0) or CycloneDX — both are the standard formats; generate one per build and keep it as the machine-readable inventory of every vendored component.
- **Gate in CI:** run the scanner on every PR, fail on disallowed/copyleft licenses reaching your proprietary tree, and regenerate the aggregate notices file so attribution can't silently rot.

### Checklist

1. Vendored code isolated under `third_party/<component>/`, unmodified where possible.
2. Per-component provenance (URL, commit, license, date, edit log).
3. Original per-file headers **intact**; `SPDX-License-Identifier` present.
4. Apache modified files carry a "changed by us" notice.
5. Top-level `LICENSE` + aggregated `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES`/`NOTICE`, CI-regenerated.
6. External names hidden behind an adapter; renaming done in the wrapper, not by defacing upstream files.
7. SCA scanner + SBOM in CI, failing the build on notice drift or license violations.

**Bottom line:** rename freely for cohesion and hide the upstream brand behind an anti-corruption layer — but treat the copyright headers, `LICENSE`, and `NOTICE` as immutable payload that rides along untouched. Cohesion is an API concern; attribution is a legal one; a clean pipeline keeps them from ever touching.

Sources: [Apache License 2.0 (§4)](https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0), [OSI MIT License](https://opensource.org/license/mit), [17 U.S.C. § 1202 (LII)](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/17/1202), [OpenStack Legal Issues FAQ (BSD/MIT header retention)](https://wiki.openstack.org/wiki/LegalIssuesFAQ), [Microsoft Learn — Anti-Corruption Layer pattern](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/anti-corruption-layer), [SPDX — Handling License Info](https://spdx.dev/learn/handling-license-info/), [SPDX short identifiers in source files](https://spdx.github.io/using/license-id-in-source/), [FOSSA — SPDX guide](https://fossa.com/learn/spdx/), [Aikido — Top OSS License Scanners 2025](https://www.aikido.dev/blog/top-open-source-license-scanners), [Linux Foundation — Using Open Source Code](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/resources/open-source-guides/using-open-source-code).
